answer:
In Cynewulf's *Andreas*, the moral valence of *wræcsīþ* (path of exile/wrath-journey) and *wræc* (exile/wrath-state) as punitive misery—banishment not merely from place but from divine favor/glory due to sin—is most vividly colored in Andreas's rebuke to Satan.[^1] The devil is told he has "dwelled in exile, deprived of glory" (*in wræc wunne, wuldres blunne*) ever since scorning God's word, his endless *wræc* an extension of torment (*susle gebunden*) with "never...an end to your exile" (*ende næfre þīnes wræces*). This frames *wræc* as retributive, a fated fall from heavenly *wuldor* (glory) into infernal isolation, echoing the poem's theme of divine justice meted on rebels (cf. the Mermedonians' *wræcsīþ* as torment-fated for unbelief: "a path of exile is fated, torment laid open").[^2]

Modern English "exile" by contrast connotes neutral relocation (political/geographical displacement), stripped of OE *wræc*'s baked-in ethical freight as covenant-breach, wrath-deserved wandering, and joyless *earfoþa* (hardship). The corpus lacks neutral *wræc*; Cynewulf's usage (here and in *Juliana*) aligns with elegiac OE precedents like *The Wanderer* (*wræclastas*: joyless, lordless paths), where exile signals relational rupture and doom.[^3]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-ca8f6e).

[^2]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-f115de).

[^3]: Cynewulf, *Juliana*, ch.1 (scattered *wræc(wræcmaegja*, *sarwræce*, etc., as vengeful torment); cf. *Old English Elegies*, "The Wanderer" [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/transliteration/#p-2ecb4b).
